title: Neoliberalism: A Bibliographic Review
author: Davies

opening_move.description: The term 'neoliberalism' has moved from a pejorative slogan to a more nuanced scholarly concept, but navigating the literature requires orientation. The paper provides a bibliographic map of the key texts that have produced a more sophisticated, historically grounded account.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A more rigorous historical account of neoliberalism distinguishes it from mere market fundamentalism, enabling better sociological analysis of how it operates today and what futures it faces.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper does not advance a position of its own but surveys and organizes existing scholarship, identifying key texts and distinguishing traditions (ordoliberalism, Chicago School, Foucauldian governmentality, post-crisis neoliberalism).

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Bibliographic synthesis and interpretive organization of secondary literature across different traditions of neoliberalism scholarship.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is a review article — its epistemic mode is unusual. It is 'fully_normative' in the sense that it does not deploy empirical material, but its function is bibliographic/synthetic rather than argumentative. The normative element is the implicit claim about what a rigorous account of neoliberalism requires.

confidence_notes: This is a bibliographic review, which is an unusual format. The epistemic mode (fully_normative) is assigned because the argument is conceptual-synthetic rather than empirical, but this paper's logic is different from the usual normative argument. The normative_stake is low and descriptive — the paper's function is survey and orientation rather than critique or prescription. The move_sequence reflects the review's structure: diagnosis of the field's confusion → mapping the literature → distinguishing traditions → genealogical tracing → agenda-setting.
